item.title: Distinct mantle metasomatism in the mantle source of ultrapotassic magma revealed by Ca Ba isotopes
item.dc:creator: Yu Chen
Yi-Xiang Chen
Xiao-Feng Gu
Riccardo Avanzinelli
Sandro Conticelli
item.description: As powerful geochemical tracers, Ca and Ba isotopes provide complementary constraints on crustal recycling, mantle metasomatism, and crust-mantle interaction. However, the extent to which Ca isotopes can uniquely track recycled carbonate-bearing components remains debated, because light Ca isotopic compositions can also be produced by process effects ( e.g. , garnet-related fractionation) and/or mineralogical controls in hydrous metasomes. In contrast, Ba isotopes have emerged as a powerful tracer of crustal recycling because Ba is highly incompatible and shows limited isotope fractionation during high-temperature magma differentiation. Here we report whole-rock Ca and Ba isotope data, together with Sr Nd isotopes and major-trace element constraints, for a suite of Italian ultrapotassic volcanic rocks spanning silica-saturated to silica-undersaturated compositions. The samples show δ 138/134 Ba values varying from −0.11‰ to 0.07‰, and generally lower δ 44/40 Ca values (0.75 ± 0.08‰) than MORB on average, with one outlier (SVK 08) exhibiting an unusually low δ 44/40 Ca value (0.43‰). Petrographic and geochemical constraints indicate that low-temperature alteration, crustal contamination, diffusion, and shallow magmatic differentiation are unlikely to dominate the Ca Ba isotope compositions. Instead, the Ca Ba isotope variations are best interpreted to reflect heterogeneity in metasomatized mantle sources. For the silica-undersaturated rocks, low δ 44/40 Ca values are consistent with contributions from Ca-rich recycled components (including carbonate-bearing sediments), and two endmember mixing and Monte Carlo calculations provide plausible bounds on sediment addition. For the silica-oversaturated lamproites, comparably low δ 44/40 Ca values can be plausibly explained by melting of a K-richterite–bearing MARID metasome, while garnet-filtered sediment/mafic metasomatic melts remain a viable alternative for extreme values. Ba isotope compositions of the samples largely reflect sediment recycling but do not uniquely resolve carbonate-specific contributions. Overall, after accounting for garnet-related fractionation, Ca isotopes are most informative for the formation of Ca-rich, silica-undersaturated rocks, whereas Ca-poor lamproites remain non-unique and require evaluation with Ba–Sr–Nd isotopes and trace-element constraints. Published: 2026-04-25T15:02:41+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.637 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Chen; Yi-Xiang Chen; Xiao-Feng Gu; Riccardo Avanzinelli; Sandro Conticelli

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123452

Relevance: 0.637 (must_read)

As powerful geochemical tracers, Ca and Ba isotopes provide complementary constraints on crustal recycling, mantle metasomatism, and crust-mantle interaction. However, the extent to which Ca isotopes can uniquely track recycled carbonate-bearing components remains debated, because light Ca isotopic compositions can also be produced by process effects ( e.g. , garnet-related fractionation) and/or mineralogical controls in hydrous metasomes. In contrast, Ba isotopes have emerged as a powerful tracer of crustal recycling because Ba is highly incompatible and shows limited isotope fractionation during high-temperature magma differentiation. Here we report whole-rock Ca and Ba isotope data, together with Sr Nd isotopes and major-trace element constraints, for a suite of Italian ultrapotassic volcanic rocks spanning silica-saturated to silica-undersaturated compositions. The samples show δ 138/134 Ba values varying from −0.11‰ to 0.07‰, and generally lower δ 44/40 Ca values (0.75 ± 0.08‰) than MORB on average, with one outlier (SVK 08) exhibiting an unusually low δ 44/40 Ca value (0.43‰). Petrographic and geochemical constraints indicate that low-temperature alteration, crustal contamination, diffusion, and shallow magmatic differentiation are unlikely to dominate the Ca Ba isotope compositions. Instead, the Ca Ba isotope variations are best interpreted to reflect heterogeneity in metasomatized mantle sources. For the silica-undersaturated rocks, low δ 44/40 Ca values are consistent with contributions from Ca-rich recycled components (including carbonate-bearing sediments), and two endmember mixing and Monte Carlo calculations provide plausible bounds on sediment addition. For the silica-oversaturated lamproites, comparably low δ 44/40 Ca values can be plausibly explained by melting of a K-richterite–bearing MARID metasome, while garnet-filtered sediment/mafic metasomatic melts remain a viable alternative for extreme values. Ba isotope compositions of the samples largely reflect sediment recycling but do not uniquely resolve carbonate-specific contributions. Overall, after accounting for garnet-related fractionation, Ca isotopes are most informative for the formation of Ca-rich, silica-undersaturated rocks, whereas Ca-poor lamproites remain non-unique and require evaluation with Ba–Sr–Nd isotopes and trace-element constraints.

item.title: Formation and exhumation of the Yudai porphyry Cu deposit in the Kalatage region, Eastern Tianshan, NW China
item.dc:creator: Lin Gong
Huayong Chen
Zhiyong Zhang
Bing Xiao
Lin Wu
item.description: As the oldest recognized porphyry Cu system in the Chinese Eastern Tianshan orogen, the Yudai Cu deposit serves as a key example for investigating how ancient porphyry copper systems formed and were preserved in the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). However, its genesis and preservation remain poorly constrained because of conflicting interpretations of mineralization age, as well as limited knowledge of magma fertility and the exhumation history of the ore-related porphyries. In order to address these issues, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of the ore-related porphyries at the Yudai deposit, including diorite porphyry and quartz diorite porphyry, through zircon U–Pb-Hf and trace element analyses, apatite U Pb dating and trace element analyses, apatite fission-track and apatite (U Th)/He dating, and thermal history reconstruction. The results indicate that the Yudai deposit experienced at least three episodes of magmatic-hydrothermal activity, occurring at ca. 453 Ma, ca. 432 Ma, and ca. 391 Ma. The long-standing discrepancy in the reported ages of the ore-bearing porphyries is best explained by sampling of different porphyritic bodies. Although the Yudai magmatic system was characterized by relatively high magmatic H 2 O contents (~5.5–6.4 wt%), which favored porphyry Cu mineralization, its relatively low oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ <0) likely limited its capacity to generate a large porphyry Cu deposit. The trace element characteristics of zircon and apatite further suggest that the diorite porphyry was comparably fertile to the ore-bearing quartz diorite porphyry. Thermal history modeling reveals differential exhumation during the Triassic (ca. 250–210 Ma) and Cretaceous (ca. 130–80 Ma), driven by fault activity. These results suggest that mineralization associated with the diorite porphyry north of the Yudai deposit may have been partly or largely removed by erosion. On this basis, future exploration should prioritize the southern and eastern parts of the Yudai district. Together with geological observations, we proposed that post-mineralization burial and later normal faulting were the main controls on the preservation of Yudai deposit. This study advances our understanding of the formation and preservation of ancient, overprinted porphyry Cu systems in the Eastern Tianshan orogen. Published: 2026-04-27T16:06:15+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.637 (must_read)

Authors: Lin Gong; Huayong Chen; Zhiyong Zhang; Bing Xiao; Lin Wu

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108090

Relevance: 0.637 (must_read)

As the oldest recognized porphyry Cu system in the Chinese Eastern Tianshan orogen, the Yudai Cu deposit serves as a key example for investigating how ancient porphyry copper systems formed and were preserved in the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). However, its genesis and preservation remain poorly constrained because of conflicting interpretations of mineralization age, as well as limited knowledge of magma fertility and the exhumation history of the ore-related porphyries. In order to address these issues, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of the ore-related porphyries at the Yudai deposit, including diorite porphyry and quartz diorite porphyry, through zircon U–Pb-Hf and trace element analyses, apatite U Pb dating and trace element analyses, apatite fission-track and apatite (U Th)/He dating, and thermal history reconstruction. The results indicate that the Yudai deposit experienced at least three episodes of magmatic-hydrothermal activity, occurring at ca. 453 Ma, ca. 432 Ma, and ca. 391 Ma. The long-standing discrepancy in the reported ages of the ore-bearing porphyries is best explained by sampling of different porphyritic bodies. Although the Yudai magmatic system was characterized by relatively high magmatic H 2 O contents (~5.5–6.4 wt%), which favored porphyry Cu mineralization, its relatively low oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ <0) likely limited its capacity to generate a large porphyry Cu deposit. The trace element characteristics of zircon and apatite further suggest that the diorite porphyry was comparably fertile to the ore-bearing quartz diorite porphyry. Thermal history modeling reveals differential exhumation during the Triassic (ca. 250–210 Ma) and Cretaceous (ca. 130–80 Ma), driven by fault activity. These results suggest that mineralization associated with the diorite porphyry north of the Yudai deposit may have been partly or largely removed by erosion. On this basis, future exploration should prioritize the southern and eastern parts of the Yudai district. Together with geological observations, we proposed that post-mineralization burial and later normal faulting were the main controls on the preservation of Yudai deposit. This study advances our understanding of the formation and preservation of ancient, overprinted porphyry Cu systems in the Eastern Tianshan orogen.

item.title: Crustal recycling and metamorphic dehydration govern the fertility of granite-associated tin systems
item.dc:creator: Xiang Sun
Huan-Chao Xu
Zhi-Ming Yang
Yong-Jun Lu
Ming-Jun Zheng
Run-Sheng Yin
Pete Hollings
Jin-Long Ma
Qing-Fei Wang
Jun Deng
item.description: Tin is a critical metal predominantly sourced from granite-associated deposits linked to anatexis of metasedimentary rocks and extreme magmatic differentiation. However, how metamorphism of sedimentary protoliths prior to crustal melting influences granite Sn fertility remains poorly constrained. Here we present an integrated boron (B) and mercury (Hg) isotope study of granites, metasedimentary basement rocks, and ores from the western Yunnan segment of the Southeast Asian Tin Belt. Tin granites and ores display Δ199Hg values of −0.41‰ to +0.06‰ and δ¹¹B values of −19.0‰ to −9.4‰, comparable to those of the basement rocks, indicating a dominant crustal volatile source. Coupled B–Hg isotopes further reveal variable volatile loss during metamorphic dehydration prior to melting, with partial melting of weakly dehydrated, volatile-rich metasedimentary protoliths favoring the generation of Sn-fertile granites. These findings highlight metamorphic preconditioning as a critical control on Sn metallogenesis and have important implications for global exploration of Sn resources. Integrated boron and mercury isotopes indicate that weakly dehydrated metasedimentary sources retain the volatiles needed to generate tin-fertile granites, highlighting metamorphic preconditioning as a key control on tin metallogenesis. Published: 2026-04-25T23:25:58+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.636 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiang Sun; Huan-Chao Xu; Zhi-Ming Yang; Yong-Jun Lu; Ming-Jun Zheng; Run-Sheng Yin; Pete Hollings; Jin-Long Ma; Qing-Fei Wang; Jun Deng

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03538-4

Relevance: 0.636 (must_read)

Tin is a critical metal predominantly sourced from granite-associated deposits linked to anatexis of metasedimentary rocks and extreme magmatic differentiation. However, how metamorphism of sedimentary protoliths prior to crustal melting influences granite Sn fertility remains poorly constrained. Here we present an integrated boron (B) and mercury (Hg) isotope study of granites, metasedimentary basement rocks, and ores from the western Yunnan segment of the Southeast Asian Tin Belt. Tin granites and ores display Δ199Hg values of −0.41‰ to +0.06‰ and δ¹¹B values of −19.0‰ to −9.4‰, comparable to those of the basement rocks, indicating a dominant crustal volatile source. Coupled B–Hg isotopes further reveal variable volatile loss during metamorphic dehydration prior to melting, with partial melting of weakly dehydrated, volatile-rich metasedimentary protoliths favoring the generation of Sn-fertile granites. These findings highlight metamorphic preconditioning as a critical control on Sn metallogenesis and have important implications for global exploration of Sn resources. Integrated boron and mercury isotopes indicate that weakly dehydrated metasedimentary sources retain the volatiles needed to generate tin-fertile granites, highlighting metamorphic preconditioning as a key control on tin metallogenesis.

item.title: Origins of Miocene basaltic magmatism in the western Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone, southern Tibet: Insight into the lateral and longitudinal tearing of the subducted Indian continental slab
item.dc:creator: Jianbo Cheng
Xianshu Dong
Yalin Li
Shuai Li
Siqi Xiao
Wenjun Bi
Rui Zhou
item.description: Post-collisional magmatic records (e.g., Oligocene–Miocene adakitic, potassic, ultrapotassic, and mafic rocks) in southern Tibet provide a unique insight into deep geodynamic processes beneath the Tibetan-Himalayan Orogen. However, the triggering mechanism and accompanied asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction for post-collisional magmatism still remain ambiguous. In this study, we present a combination of geological, petrological, geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic data for newly-identified Middle Miocene basaltic rocks in the Zhari and Kongmeda areas in the Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone (YTSZ). The Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks have a medium-K trachybasalt affinity and oceanic island basalt (OIB)-type geochemical characteristics with moderate La N /Yb N ratios (6.69–17.82 and 5.18–8.90) and slightly positive Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies. They also show relatively depleted Sr Nd isotopic compositions with initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr (0.7058–0.7064, 0.7082–0.7092) and positive ε Nd (t) (+2.7 to +3.1, +1.2 to +2.4). Geochemical signatures and modeling results demonstrate that the Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks were most likely generated by low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric mantle sources at or slightly below the ~75–85-km-deep garnet–spinel transition zone, along with minor involvements of lithospheric mantle components during magma ascent, implying asthenospheric upwelling and resultant asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction beneath the western YTSZ. Based on large-scale spatiotemporal analysis of post-collisional magmatic records in southern Tibet, the E–W-trending post-collisional magmatic zone with bidirectional center-ward younging trend was predominantly triggered by the bilateral tearing and detachment of the subducted Indian continental slab, along with minor effects from its longitudinal tearing, during the Oligocene–Miocene. Published: 2026-04-28T06:51:49+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.626 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jianbo Cheng; Xianshu Dong; Yalin Li; Shuai Li; Siqi Xiao; Wenjun Bi; Rui Zhou

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123450

Relevance: 0.626 (consider)

Post-collisional magmatic records (e.g., Oligocene–Miocene adakitic, potassic, ultrapotassic, and mafic rocks) in southern Tibet provide a unique insight into deep geodynamic processes beneath the Tibetan-Himalayan Orogen. However, the triggering mechanism and accompanied asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction for post-collisional magmatism still remain ambiguous. In this study, we present a combination of geological, petrological, geochronological, geochemical, and isotopic data for newly-identified Middle Miocene basaltic rocks in the Zhari and Kongmeda areas in the Yarlung-Tsangpo Suture Zone (YTSZ). The Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks have a medium-K trachybasalt affinity and oceanic island basalt (OIB)-type geochemical characteristics with moderate La N /Yb N ratios (6.69–17.82 and 5.18–8.90) and slightly positive Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies. They also show relatively depleted Sr Nd isotopic compositions with initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr (0.7058–0.7064, 0.7082–0.7092) and positive ε Nd (t) (+2.7 to +3.1, +1.2 to +2.4). Geochemical signatures and modeling results demonstrate that the Zhari and Kongmeda basaltic rocks were most likely generated by low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric mantle sources at or slightly below the ~75–85-km-deep garnet–spinel transition zone, along with minor involvements of lithospheric mantle components during magma ascent, implying asthenospheric upwelling and resultant asthenosphere-lithosphere interaction beneath the western YTSZ. Based on large-scale spatiotemporal analysis of post-collisional magmatic records in southern Tibet, the E–W-trending post-collisional magmatic zone with bidirectional center-ward younging trend was predominantly triggered by the bilateral tearing and detachment of the subducted Indian continental slab, along with minor effects from its longitudinal tearing, during the Oligocene–Miocene.

item.title: Molybdenum isotopic compositions in volcanic rocks from Northeast Japan
item.dc:creator: Tatsuya Tamura
Tetsuya Yokoyama
James B. Gill
Takeshi Kuritani
Hikaru Iwamori
Kenta Ueki
Takashi Kudo
item.description: The Mo isotope ratio ( δ 98/95 Mo value) of subduction zone volcanic rocks has been used as a valuable tracer to elucidate material transport by dehydration of aqueous fluid from the slab and recycling of the dehydrated slab deep in the mantle. However, while some volcanoes, such as oceanic island basalts and continental basalts, have been extensively investigated, studies on across-arc variations from the volcanic front (VF) to the rear arc (RA) in volcanic arcs have been limited. The Northeastern Japan (NEJ) arc, a volcanic arc with a well-developed crust and a large number of volcanoes distributed across the VF to RA regions, is particularly suitable for studying geochemical variations along with the depth of Wadati-Benioff zone. Published: 2026-04-24T23:26:57+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.624 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tatsuya Tamura; Tetsuya Yokoyama; James B. Gill; Takeshi Kuritani; Hikaru Iwamori; Kenta Ueki; Takashi Kudo

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.025

Relevance: 0.624 (consider)

The Mo isotope ratio ( δ 98/95 Mo value) of subduction zone volcanic rocks has been used as a valuable tracer to elucidate material transport by dehydration of aqueous fluid from the slab and recycling of the dehydrated slab deep in the mantle. However, while some volcanoes, such as oceanic island basalts and continental basalts, have been extensively investigated, studies on across-arc variations from the volcanic front (VF) to the rear arc (RA) in volcanic arcs have been limited. The Northeastern Japan (NEJ) arc, a volcanic arc with a well-developed crust and a large number of volcanoes distributed across the VF to RA regions, is particularly suitable for studying geochemical variations along with the depth of Wadati-Benioff zone.

item.title: Epidote Records (De)hydration From the Seafloor Through Subduction and Underplating: Implications for Subduction Zone Fluid Budgets and Slow Slip
item.dc:creator: P. C. Lindquist
C. B. Condit
W. F. Hoover
V. E. Guevara
G. S. Epstein
item.description: Abstract When, where, and how much fluid is released from subducting oceanic crust control deformation, element cycling, and magmatism in subduction zones. However, the palimpsest of tectonometamorphic processes in the exhumed rock record complicates the relation of geologic observations to geodynamic models of subduction fluid sources and transport. We present textural observations and major and trace element geochemical measurements of epidote combined with phase equilibrium modeling to decipher the pressure‐temperature and dehydration history of metabasalts from the Catalina Schist Epidote Amphibolite Unit (California, USA). We distinguish four generations of epidotes, representing (a) hydrothermal alteration of pillow basalts, (b) prograde epidote formation and fluid release during pumpellyite breakdown at 290–310°C and 0.4–0.7 GPa, (c) equilibration at peak P‐T conditions of 550–600°C and 0.6–0.9 GPa, and (d) retrograde epidote (re)growth during underplating and refrigeration. Geochemical data suggest that basalts were hydrated at the seafloor, and during subduction, metabasalts experienced a large pulse of fluid release during pumpellyite breakdown, recorded by epidote‐rich self‐organizing porosity structures, which helped to drain fluid from the subducting slab. This water was released from the subducting slab at depths corresponding to the base of the seismogenic zone and the shallowest depths at which deep slow slip and tremor occur in modern subduction zones, providing a geologic example of local metamorphic dehydration contributing to the maintenance of high pore fluid pressures at the plate interface. We show that epidote can serve as a recorder of progressive metamorphic processes when careful consideration of textures and mineral chemistry is applied. Published: 2026-04-27T04:22:23+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.623 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: P. C. Lindquist; C. B. Condit; W. F. Hoover; V. E. Guevara; G. S. Epstein

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012862

Relevance: 0.623 (consider)

Abstract When, where, and how much fluid is released from subducting oceanic crust control deformation, element cycling, and magmatism in subduction zones. However, the palimpsest of tectonometamorphic processes in the exhumed rock record complicates the relation of geologic observations to geodynamic models of subduction fluid sources and transport. We present textural observations and major and trace element geochemical measurements of epidote combined with phase equilibrium modeling to decipher the pressure‐temperature and dehydration history of metabasalts from the Catalina Schist Epidote Amphibolite Unit (California, USA). We distinguish four generations of epidotes, representing (a) hydrothermal alteration of pillow basalts, (b) prograde epidote formation and fluid release during pumpellyite breakdown at 290–310°C and 0.4–0.7 GPa, (c) equilibration at peak P‐T conditions of 550–600°C and 0.6–0.9 GPa, and (d) retrograde epidote (re)growth during underplating and refrigeration. Geochemical data suggest that basalts were hydrated at the seafloor, and during subduction, metabasalts experienced a large pulse of fluid release during pumpellyite breakdown, recorded by epidote‐rich self‐organizing porosity structures, which helped to drain fluid from the subducting slab. This water was released from the subducting slab at depths corresponding to the base of the seismogenic zone and the shallowest depths at which deep slow slip and tremor occur in modern subduction zones, providing a geologic example of local metamorphic dehydration contributing to the maintenance of high pore fluid pressures at the plate interface. We show that epidote can serve as a recorder of progressive metamorphic processes when careful consideration of textures and mineral chemistry is applied.

item.title: Lower-mantle iron heterogeneity constrained by the electrical conductivity of Al-bearing bridgmanite
item.dc:creator: Kui Han
Sinan Özaydın
Hongzhan Fei
Lianjie Man
Fei Wang
Artem Chanyshev
Anthony C. Withers
Alexander Grayver
Tomoo Katsura
item.description: Iron distribution in Earth’s lower mantle profoundly influences planetary evolution by regulating mineral density and mantle dynamics but remains poorly constrained due to trade-offs between temperature and composition in seismic interpretations. Here, we resolve this challenge by measuring electrical conductivity of Al-, Fe-bearing bridgmanite, the dominant lower-mantle mineral, as a function of iron content ( X Fe ) under conditions reaching 2000 K and 27 GPa. Bridgmanite conductivity increases dramatically with X Fe following an X Fe 3.6 power law, while showing minimal temperature dependence. This pronounced sensitivity enables direct inference of global iron variation from geomagnetic-derived conductivity models. Our analysis reveals iron enrichment in large low–shear velocity provinces, supporting their thermochemical rather than purely thermal nature. We identify extensive high-iron regions beneath the western Pacific extending below 1000 km, indicating a vast basaltic reservoir of subducted oceanic crust. These findings provide independent constraints on Earth’s chemical composition and evolution. Published: 2026-04-24T17:58:32+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.609 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Kui Han; Sinan Özaydın; Hongzhan Fei; Lianjie Man; Fei Wang; Artem Chanyshev; Anthony C. Withers; Alexander Grayver; Tomoo Katsura

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aec7875

Relevance: 0.609 (consider)

Iron distribution in Earth’s lower mantle profoundly influences planetary evolution by regulating mineral density and mantle dynamics but remains poorly constrained due to trade-offs between temperature and composition in seismic interpretations. Here, we resolve this challenge by measuring electrical conductivity of Al-, Fe-bearing bridgmanite, the dominant lower-mantle mineral, as a function of iron content ( X Fe ) under conditions reaching 2000 K and 27 GPa. Bridgmanite conductivity increases dramatically with X Fe following an X Fe 3.6 power law, while showing minimal temperature dependence. This pronounced sensitivity enables direct inference of global iron variation from geomagnetic-derived conductivity models. Our analysis reveals iron enrichment in large low–shear velocity provinces, supporting their thermochemical rather than purely thermal nature. We identify extensive high-iron regions beneath the western Pacific extending below 1000 km, indicating a vast basaltic reservoir of subducted oceanic crust. These findings provide independent constraints on Earth’s chemical composition and evolution.

item.title: Controls on Mg isotopic fractionation between deep mantle phases and relict signatures of a terrestrial magma ocean
item.dc:creator: Andrew M. Walker
Remco C. Hin
Tim Elliott
item.description: We use density functional theory to investigate the fractionation of Mg isotopes between phases in the lower mantle. Our results support previous work and show that coordination number plays an important role in controlling isotopic fractionation, with bridgmanite (perovskite-structured MgSiO 3 ) preferentially incorporating lighter Mg isotopes into its highly coordinated site compared to periclase (MgO). Increasing pressure enhances this fractionation while increasing temperature decreases it. These two effects trade-off such that the preference is evident across all lower mantle conditions explored, even to the high temperatures of the chondritic liquidus (e.g. Δ 26 / 24 " role="presentation"> Δ 26 / 24 Δ 26 / 24 Mg Per−Bdm " role="presentation"> Per−Bdm Per−Bdm is 0.04‰ at 4200 K and 87 GPa). In additional numerical experiments we separate the effect of coordination number from differences in bond length between different phases and these allow us to build an ionic model which parameterises magnesium isotope fractionation as a function of bond length, coordination number, and temperature. This model provides us with a preliminary means to describe isotope partitioning between solid and liquid phases when making predictions of Mg isotopic differences generated during terrestrial magma ocean crystallisation. We find that Mg isotopic fractionation between bridgmanite and melt, at lower mantle conditions, is sufficient to generate detectable differences in the Mg isotopic compositions of a residual melt or solid cumulate phase, relative to bulk Earth. More specifically, we show that isolation of a reservoir of cumulate bridgmanite that is some 3%–15% by mass of the mantle could account for the super-chondritic 26 Mg/ 24 Mg of accessible terrestrial peridotite samples. Whether such a reservoir can be dynamically preserved over Earth history remains an open question, but our results help quantify possible tests of such a scenario. Published: 2026-04-25T15:03:28+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.602 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Andrew M. Walker; Remco C. Hin; Tim Elliott

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.030

Relevance: 0.602 (consider)

We use density functional theory to investigate the fractionation of Mg isotopes between phases in the lower mantle. Our results support previous work and show that coordination number plays an important role in controlling isotopic fractionation, with bridgmanite (perovskite-structured MgSiO 3 ) preferentially incorporating lighter Mg isotopes into its highly coordinated site compared to periclase (MgO). Increasing pressure enhances this fractionation while increasing temperature decreases it. These two effects trade-off such that the preference is evident across all lower mantle conditions explored, even to the high temperatures of the chondritic liquidus (e.g. Δ 26 / 24 " role="presentation"> Δ 26 / 24 Δ 26 / 24 Mg Per−Bdm " role="presentation"> Per−Bdm Per−Bdm is 0.04‰ at 4200 K and 87 GPa). In additional numerical experiments we separate the effect of coordination number from differences in bond length between different phases and these allow us to build an ionic model which parameterises magnesium isotope fractionation as a function of bond length, coordination number, and temperature. This model provides us with a preliminary means to describe isotope partitioning between solid and liquid phases when making predictions of Mg isotopic differences generated during terrestrial magma ocean crystallisation. We find that Mg isotopic fractionation between bridgmanite and melt, at lower mantle conditions, is sufficient to generate detectable differences in the Mg isotopic compositions of a residual melt or solid cumulate phase, relative to bulk Earth. More specifically, we show that isolation of a reservoir of cumulate bridgmanite that is some 3%–15% by mass of the mantle could account for the super-chondritic 26 Mg/ 24 Mg of accessible terrestrial peridotite samples. Whether such a reservoir can be dynamically preserved over Earth history remains an open question, but our results help quantify possible tests of such a scenario.

item.title: Enigmatic postsubduction lavas from a fossil slab remnant beneath Baja California, Mexico
item.dc:creator: James K. Muller
Emily J. Chin
item.description: Geochemically unusual, low-volume lavas have erupted along the Baja California Peninsula (BCP) in Mexico since the Miocene. These rocks have remarkably high Sr contents and Sr/Y ratios. High-Sr/Y magmas are commonly ascribed to partial melting of subducting young oceanic crust (adakites) or melting of mantle previously metasomatized by slab melts (bajaites). However, BCP adakites and bajaites erupted after Farallon plate subduction ended locally. We leverage a novel approach to studying the Baja Californian adakites by combining phase equilibria and trace element modeling. Our calculated melts of a relict slab fragment (imaged geophysically beneath the BCP) are consistent with the trace element compositions of local adakites but only at high pressures when residues comprise garnet-bearing eclogite. We infer the high thermal regime needed to melt the fossil slab and overlying metasomatized mantle may originate via shear heating, sub-slab mantle flow, or a combination thereof. Recent studies have invoked slab melting to reconcile geochemical mass balance at active arcs. The high-Sr/Y BCP lavas may offer a unique, isolated view into this slab-derived component because dominant flux melting overwhelms such a signal in active subduction zones. Published: 2026-04-28T17:10:32+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.600 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: James K. Muller; Emily J. Chin

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g53817.1

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

Geochemically unusual, low-volume lavas have erupted along the Baja California Peninsula (BCP) in Mexico since the Miocene. These rocks have remarkably high Sr contents and Sr/Y ratios. High-Sr/Y magmas are commonly ascribed to partial melting of subducting young oceanic crust (adakites) or melting of mantle previously metasomatized by slab melts (bajaites). However, BCP adakites and bajaites erupted after Farallon plate subduction ended locally. We leverage a novel approach to studying the Baja Californian adakites by combining phase equilibria and trace element modeling. Our calculated melts of a relict slab fragment (imaged geophysically beneath the BCP) are consistent with the trace element compositions of local adakites but only at high pressures when residues comprise garnet-bearing eclogite. We infer the high thermal regime needed to melt the fossil slab and overlying metasomatized mantle may originate via shear heating, sub-slab mantle flow, or a combination thereof. Recent studies have invoked slab melting to reconcile geochemical mass balance at active arcs. The high-Sr/Y BCP lavas may offer a unique, isolated view into this slab-derived component because dominant flux melting overwhelms such a signal in active subduction zones.

item.title: A step metallogenic model and mechanism for hydrothermal vein-type gold deposits: A case study from the Jiaodong gold province
item.dc:creator: Mingchun Song
Zhiming Yang
Jie Li
Jian Li
Runsheng Wang
Chunyan He
Zhenliang Yang
Junjin Zhang
Tianci Xie
item.description: Hydrothermal mineralization systems and the geometric distribution of orebodies are structurally controlled. The step metallogenic model previously proposed, based on the structural ore-controlling patterns of the Jiaodong gold province, has provided strong support for breakthroughs in deep prospecting. However, this model still lacks systematic validation through deep observational data and does not adequately explain the formation mechanism of gently dipping, large, and thick orebodies. This study, through detailed statistical analysis of dip angle variations in orebodies and ore-controlling fractures from drill-hole data of typical Jiaodong gold deposits, as well as geophysical detection and inversion revealing deep fault structures and the coupling relationship between orebodies and faults, systematically reveals the ore-controlling patterns within gently dipping fault segments and the characteristics of stepwise mineralization. It indicates that gently dipping orebodies are the main contributors to the enormous gold endowment in Jiaodong Peninsula. Comparative analysis with other typical gold deposits in China suggests that large, thick, and high-grade gold orebodies are distributed in multiple layers from shallow to deep depths, constituting three forms of step metallogenic models: (i) orebodies hosted in the steep-to-gentle dip transition zones and the relatively gentle segments of the same fault; (ii) orebodies occurring in gently dipping secondary fractures adjacent to steeply dipping major faults; and (iii) orebodies controlled by gently dipping interlayer structures within strata bounded by multiple steeply dipping faults. Research indicates that rocks near gently dipping faults have weaker shear fracture resistance and are prone to stress concentration, while the faults themselves exhibit better sealing properties, which are conducive to fluid convergence and precipitation. Within the tectonic setting of the Jiaodong detachment fault system and with granitoids as the main host rocks, a fault dip angle of around 30° is most favorable for forming large ore-hosting spaces. The stepwise metallogenic model holds significant application value for deep gold exploration. Published: 2026-04-28T02:58:33+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.593 (consider)

Journal: Science China Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1007/s11430-025-1876-0

Relevance: 0.593 (consider)

Hydrothermal mineralization systems and the geometric distribution of orebodies are structurally controlled. The step metallogenic model previously proposed, based on the structural ore-controlling patterns of the Jiaodong gold province, has provided strong support for breakthroughs in deep prospecting. However, this model still lacks systematic validation through deep observational data and does not adequately explain the formation mechanism of gently dipping, large, and thick orebodies. This study, through detailed statistical analysis of dip angle variations in orebodies and ore-controlling fractures from drill-hole data of typical Jiaodong gold deposits, as well as geophysical detection and inversion revealing deep fault structures and the coupling relationship between orebodies and faults, systematically reveals the ore-controlling patterns within gently dipping fault segments and the characteristics of stepwise mineralization. It indicates that gently dipping orebodies are the main contributors to the enormous gold endowment in Jiaodong Peninsula. Comparative analysis with other typical gold deposits in China suggests that large, thick, and high-grade gold orebodies are distributed in multiple layers from shallow to deep depths, constituting three forms of step metallogenic models: (i) orebodies hosted in the steep-to-gentle dip transition zones and the relatively gentle segments of the same fault; (ii) orebodies occurring in gently dipping secondary fractures adjacent to steeply dipping major faults; and (iii) orebodies controlled by gently dipping interlayer structures within strata bounded by multiple steeply dipping faults. Research indicates that rocks near gently dipping faults have weaker shear fracture resistance and are prone to stress concentration, while the faults themselves exhibit better sealing properties, which are conducive to fluid convergence and precipitation. Within the tectonic setting of the Jiaodong detachment fault system and with granitoids as the main host rocks, a fault dip angle of around 30° is most favorable for forming large ore-hosting spaces. The stepwise metallogenic model holds significant application value for deep gold exploration.

item.title: Integrating detrital magnetite geochemistry and (U Th)/He chronometry as a sediment provenance tool in geologic and metallogenic terranes
item.dc:creator: Robert G. McDermott
Douglas C. Kreiner
James V. Jones
Sean P. Regan
James R. Metcalf
Rebecca M. Flowers
item.description: Magnetite is ubiquitous in porphyry Cu systems and in sediment sourcing both barren and mineralized regions, with potential as an indicator mineral in concealed and coarsely-mapped terranes. We develop and test a workflow for integrated geochemistry and (U Th)/He (He) dating for inferring detrital magnetite (DMt) provenance in these settings. The ca. 70 Ma Taurus porphyry Cu-Mo( Au) district in eastern interior Alaska serves as a test case. DMt from streams draining porphyry-related mineralization was characterized by geochemistry and mineral inclusion and microstructure observations, complemented by similar data for potential porphyry and host rock sources. Principal component analysis and clustering of DMt geochemical data resolve multiple populations in our samples geochemically and texturally compatible with derivation from metamorphic, porphyry-related hydrothermal, and igneous sources. Hydrothermal magnetite comprises ~16–50% of DMt nearest porphyry mineralization but diminishes to ~4% ~15 km downstream. Subsampled grains within populations yield ~160–110 Ma, ~70 Ma, ~55 Ma, and ~ 20 Ma magnetite He date modes. Combined with provenance, He dates capture Early Cretaceous regional exhumation of metamorphic host rock and Late Cretaceous porphyry Cu mineralization. DMt grains showing partial hematite replacement yield ca. 55–20 Ma dates regardless of source, overlapping regional warm/wet climatic intervals. We interpret Cenozoic dates to reflect exhumation to near-surface oxidizing conditions and(or) supergene weathering. Magnetite is thus a promising target phase for (1) tracking the spatiotemporal distribution of porphyry systems, and (2) linking the formation and exhumation of these systems to a regional geologic history, both in Alaska and globally. Published: 2026-04-24T15:05:50+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.590 (consider)

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123448

Relevance: 0.590 (consider)

Magnetite is ubiquitous in porphyry Cu systems and in sediment sourcing both barren and mineralized regions, with potential as an indicator mineral in concealed and coarsely-mapped terranes. We develop and test a workflow for integrated geochemistry and (U Th)/He (He) dating for inferring detrital magnetite (DMt) provenance in these settings. The ca. 70 Ma Taurus porphyry Cu-Mo( Au) district in eastern interior Alaska serves as a test case. DMt from streams draining porphyry-related mineralization was characterized by geochemistry and mineral inclusion and microstructure observations, complemented by similar data for potential porphyry and host rock sources. Principal component analysis and clustering of DMt geochemical data resolve multiple populations in our samples geochemically and texturally compatible with derivation from metamorphic, porphyry-related hydrothermal, and igneous sources. Hydrothermal magnetite comprises ~16–50% of DMt nearest porphyry mineralization but diminishes to ~4% ~15 km downstream. Combined with provenance, He dates capture Early Cretaceous regional exhumation of metamorphic host rock and Late Cretaceous porphyry Cu mineralization. DMt grains showing partial hematite replacement yield ca. 55–20 Ma dates regardless of source, overlapping regional warm/wet climatic intervals. We interpret Cenozoic dates to reflect exhumation to near-surface oxidizing conditions and(or) supergene weathering. Magnetite is thus a promising target phase for (1) tracking the spatiotemporal distribution of porphyry systems, and (2) linking the formation and exhumation of these systems to a regional geologic history, both in Alaska and globally.

item.title: Tracing Mesoarchean crust-mantle interaction processes using Mo–Sr–Nd–Hf isotopes in TTGs and Nb-enriched basalts in the Kongling complex, Yangtze Craton
item.dc:creator: Peiwen Liu
Jibiao Zhang
Xiaozhong Ding
Yanxue Liu
Tingdong Li
item.description: The crust–mantle interactions and geodynamic regimes of late Archean continental crust formation and evolution are a hot topic in global Precambrian research. As an important component of the early continental crust, tonalite, trondhjemite, and granodiorite (TTG) suites preserve key information on crust formation and evolution, but their origin remains a subject of intense debate. Here, we report zircon U Pb ages, major and trace element data, and Mo–Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic compositions for Mesoarchean TTGs and associated Nb-enriched basalts from the Kongling Complex, Yangtze Craton. The ~2.98 Ga Shuiyuesi TTGs are characterized by high Mg# values (47–55), high Sr/Y (58.4–63.9), and (La/Yb) N ratios (32.6–98.2), low Mo contents, low δ 98/95 Mo values (−0.69‰ to −0.52‰; relative to the NIST SRM 3134 standard), and depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures. These characteristics suggest that they were derived from the partial melting of a residual oceanic slab, which lost isotopically heavy Mo during dehydration. Similar to the Shuiyuesi TTGs, the ~2.95 Ga Maoya TTGs also show high Mg# values (44–57), high Sr/Y (46.7–118.6), and (La/Yb) N ratios (72.6–158.7). However, they exhibit higher δ 98/95 Mo (−0.13‰ to −0.04‰) values and more enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic ratios than the Shuiyuesi TTGs, indicating that they were derived from the partial melting of ancient crustal source with heavy Mo isotopic compositions. The ~2.88 Ga Nb-enriched basalts are characterized by arc-type trace element signatures but display higher TiO 2 , P 2 O 5 , Nb, Nb/La, and Nb/U values than normal arc volcanics. They show low δ 98/95 Mo (−0.46‰ to −0.25‰) values and highly depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures, suggesting that they were formed by partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by melts derived from dehydrated oceanic crust. Considering the complex crust–mantle processes during the formation of TTGs and Nb-enriched basalts, we suggest that the Mesoarchean lithological assemblages in the Kongling Complex were most likely formed in an active continental margin setting. Our research also indicates that Mo isotopes can effectively trace the source of Archean TTG rocks as well as crust–mantle interactions. Published: 2026-04-24T15:05:50+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.590 (consider)

Authors: Peiwen Liu; Jibiao Zhang; Xiaozhong Ding; Yanxue Liu; Tingdong Li

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123446

Relevance: 0.590 (consider)

The crust–mantle interactions and geodynamic regimes of late Archean continental crust formation and evolution are a hot topic in global Precambrian research. As an important component of the early continental crust, tonalite, trondhjemite, and granodiorite (TTG) suites preserve key information on crust formation and evolution, but their origin remains a subject of intense debate. Here, we report zircon U Pb ages, major and trace element data, and Mo–Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic compositions for Mesoarchean TTGs and associated Nb-enriched basalts from the Kongling Complex, Yangtze Craton. The ~2.98 Ga Shuiyuesi TTGs are characterized by high Mg# values (47–55), high Sr/Y (58.4–63.9), and (La/Yb) N ratios (32.6–98.2), low Mo contents, low δ 98/95 Mo values (−0.69‰ to −0.52‰; relative to the NIST SRM 3134 standard), and depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures. These characteristics suggest that they were derived from the partial melting of a residual oceanic slab, which lost isotopically heavy Mo during dehydration. Similar to the Shuiyuesi TTGs, the ~2.95 Ga Maoya TTGs also show high Mg# values (44–57), high Sr/Y (46.7–118.6), and (La/Yb) N ratios (72.6–158.7). However, they exhibit higher δ 98/95 Mo (−0.13‰ to −0.04‰) values and more enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic ratios than the Shuiyuesi TTGs, indicating that they were derived from the partial melting of ancient crustal source with heavy Mo isotopic compositions. The ~2.88 Ga Nb-enriched basalts are characterized by arc-type trace element signatures but display higher TiO 2 , P 2 O 5 , Nb, Nb/La, and Nb/U values than normal arc volcanics. They show low δ 98/95 Mo (−0.46‰ to −0.25‰) values and highly depleted Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic signatures, suggesting that they were formed by partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by melts derived from dehydrated oceanic crust. Considering the complex crust–mantle processes during the formation of TTGs and Nb-enriched basalts, we suggest that the Mesoarchean lithological assemblages in the Kongling Complex were most likely formed in an active continental margin setting. Our research also indicates that Mo isotopes can effectively trace the source of Archean TTG rocks as well as crust–mantle interactions.

item.title: The granite-pegmatite-associated Marvel Loch gold deposit, Yilgarn Craton, Western Australia: magnesian and calcic gold skarns in a 1.5 km long contact zone
item.dc:creator: Andreas G. Mueller
item.description: Marvel Loch (63 t Au) represents a group of high-P (300–400 MPa) gold deposits spatially associated with 2.65–2.62 Ga, garnet-muscovite and spodumene pegmatites in the 3.0 Ga Southern Cross greenstone belt, Yilgarn Craton. The ore bodies terminate at 650–800 m depth at pegmatitic granite. Structural relations, mass-balance calculations, 87Sr/86Sr and 206Pb/204Pb in scheelite and galena, and the δ13CPDB of CO2 (-4.8‰) in the calcite-depositing fluid implicate the I-type granite below as the source. Magnesian olivine-calcite-chlorite-phlogopite, quartz-diopside, and calcic diopside-hornblende skarn (4–15 g/t Au), and low-grade biotite-anorthite-microcline gneiss occur in a 1.5 km long foliated zone bound to the contact meta-komatiite / meta-gabbro. Garnet-biotite and Ti-in-biotite thermometry indicate a peak temperature of 650 –550 °C during calcic skarn and biotite-anorthite-microcline replacement in meta-gabbro, and cooling to about 400 °C during retrograde alteration of anorthite to phengite + prehnite + clinozoisite, confirming estimates for magnesian skarn. The fluid (log fO2 = -18 bar at 650 °C) became reduced during cooling and progressive replacement, as suggested by Fe3+/Fetotal ratios in whole rocks, CH4 in fluid inclusions, and positive Eu-anomalies in scheelite. At a sulfur fugacity of 10− 7-10− 9 bar, cooling is reflected in pyrrhotite + Ni-loellingite (600 –500 °C) in calcite-rich magnesian skarn, arsenopyrite + pyrrhotite + base-metal sulfides ± bismuth (550 –400 °C) in quartz-diopside skarn, and retrograde pyrrhotite + pyrite ± marcasite (450 –350 °C) in metasomatic gneiss. Marvel Loch is a gold skarn deposit formed at 11–15 km depth in a batholith environment. Reduced tungsten skarns (7–10 km) share the W-Au-As-Bi signature, and the reduction of a magmatic H2O-CO2 fluid during cooling and replacement of ferrous or graphitic host rocks. Published: 2026-04-28T02:26:59+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.589 (consider)

Marvel Loch (63 t Au) represents a group of high-P (300–400 MPa) gold deposits spatially associated with 2.65–2.62 Ga, garnet-muscovite and spodumene pegmatites in the 3.0 Ga Southern Cross greenstone belt, Yilgarn Craton. The ore bodies terminate at 650–800 m depth at pegmatitic granite. Structural relations, mass-balance calculations, 87Sr/86Sr and 206Pb/204Pb in scheelite and galena, and the δ13CPDB of CO2 (-4.8‰) in the calcite-depositing fluid implicate the I-type granite below as the source. Garnet-biotite and Ti-in-biotite thermometry indicate a peak temperature of 650 –550 °C during calcic skarn and biotite-anorthite-microcline replacement in meta-gabbro, and cooling to about 400 °C during retrograde alteration of anorthite to phengite + prehnite + clinozoisite, confirming estimates for magnesian skarn. The fluid (log fO2 = -18 bar at 650 °C) became reduced during cooling and progressive replacement, as suggested by Fe3+/Fetotal ratios in whole rocks, CH4 in fluid inclusions, and positive Eu-anomalies in scheelite. At a sulfur fugacity of 10− 7-10− 9 bar, cooling is reflected in pyrrhotite + Ni-loellingite (600 –500 °C) in calcite-rich magnesian skarn, arsenopyrite + pyrrhotite + base-metal sulfides ± bismuth (550 –400 °C) in quartz-diopside skarn, and retrograde pyrrhotite + pyrite ± marcasite (450 –350 °C) in metasomatic gneiss. Reduced tungsten skarns (7–10 km) share the W-Au-As-Bi signature, and the reduction of a magmatic H2O-CO2 fluid during cooling and replacement of ferrous or graphitic host rocks.

item.title: Tracing deep Earth volatile heterogeneities with heavy noble gases in Réunion plume-influenced Central Indian Ridge basalts
item.dc:creator: Xinmu J. Zhang
Rita Parai
Peter H. Barry
Evelyn Füri
item.description: The initial budgets and subsequent exchange of volatiles ( e.g ., water, carbon, nitrogen, and noble gases) among surface and deep reservoirs govern planetary surface conditions and long-term habitability. Varieties of modern terrestrial basalts—mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORBs), which derive primarily from melting of Earth’s upper mantle, and plume-influenced basalts that sample the lower mantle—provide access to two distinct volatile reservoirs within Earth’s interior. Their heavy noble gas (Ne, Ar, Kr, and Xe) isotope systematics offer powerful insights into volatile accretion, outgassing, and recycling histories of different terrestrial mantle reservoirs. However, existing high-precision heavy noble gas datasets are limited due to challenges related to sample access and analytical methods. Basaltic glasses from the Central Indian Ridge (CIR), influenced by the nearby Réunion hotspot, provide an opportunity to expand the global heavy noble gas record. Here, we present high precision He, Ne, Ar, and Xe elemental and isotopic data measured from four submarine basalts collected from the CIR axis between 16.7°S and 20.6°S. The resulting heavy noble gas dataset confirms Réunion plume influence at the CIR axis near ∼20°S and provides constraints on the origins of the region’s compositional variations. The mantle source Xe composition of the Réunion-influenced CIR basalt D14–1 is consistent with observations from other plume-influenced localities, suggesting globally uniform plume-source volatile origins, with early-formed isotopic signatures preserved from accretion to the present-day. Published: 2026-04-28T12:55:52+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.581 (consider)

item.title: Quartz geochemical constraints on fluid evolution during low- to medium-temperature hydrothermal Sb and Au mineralization
item.dc:creator: Shen Gao
Xinyu Zou
Liangliang Huang
Hongye Feng
Jiajun Liu
Degao Zhai
Kezhang Qin
Guangming Li
item.description: Hydrothermal systems related to antimony (Sb) and gold (Au) ores form under low- to medium-temperature conditions (150−350 °C) and are often associated with major ore deposit belts. Ongoing debate surrounds the characteristics of ore-forming fluids, which are influenced by fluid sources, fluid-rock reactions, and evolutionary processes. Trace elements in quartz are critical for understanding fluid evolution in magmatic-hydrothermal systems (e.g., porphyry-epithermal deposits). This study analyzed trace elements in quartz from 43 Sb and Au deposits worldwide, focusing on low- to medium-temperature conditions. The deposits included Mesozoic Sb belts, Carlin-type and intrusion-related Au provinces in China, and late Paleozoic Sb-Au deposits in Europe. The findings reveal that Ti-Al geochemical diagrams indicate distinct affinities for the host rocks. Specifically, systems hosted by igneous rocks display elevated Ti/Al ratios (>0.005), whereas those hosted by sedimentary rocks exhibit lower ratios (<0.005). Metamorphic rock−hosted systems are best characterized by Sb-As plots and a ternary diagram involving (Ge + As)−Ti × 5−Sb. The observed relationships between Sb and As suggest that trends in Sb mineralization are more closely aligned with metamorphic rock−hosted systems, whereas trends in Au mineralization are associated with igneous rock−hosted systems in the current dataset. Sedimentary rock−hosted systems show quartz trace-element trends indicative of both Sb and Au mineralization. The Li-Al trends further highlight the distinct fluid evolution associated with Sb and Au mineralization, particularly in vein-type Sb, intrusion-related Au, and Carlin-type Au deposits. Variations in trace elements within quartz are attributed to compositional changes induced by cooling. Our results demonstrate that the trace-element signatures identified in quartz provide new insights into the evolution of fluids in low- to medium-temperature hydrothermal Sb and Au systems. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:27+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.578 (consider)

item.title: Hadean components preserved in Paleo- to Neoarchean rocks from the Yilgarn Craton, W-Australia
item.dc:creator: Max Hellers
Jonas Tusch
Carina Gerritzen
Eric Hasenstab-Dübler
Mario Fischer-Gödde
Andreas R.A. Schneider
Chris S. Marien
Josua J. Pakulla
R. Hugh Smithies
Martin J. Van Kranendonk
Dieter Garbe-Schönberg
Carsten Münker
item.description: The Narryer Terrane is known for its Hadean zircons, demonstrating the existence of evolved Hadean crust in the Yilgarn Craton (W-Australia). However, the effects of Hadean material on long- and short-lived isotope signatures have remained ambiguous. We present the first combined 146 Sm- 142 Nd and 182 Hf- 182 W dataset for rocks from the Yilgarn Craton to identify putative Hadean components in crustal and mantle reservoirs through time. We investigated these short-lived isotope systems in mafic-ultramafic sequences, metasediments and granitoids covering the oldest preserved Paleoarchean rocks up to the Neoarchean granitoids that mark the stabilisation of the craton. Our study reveals modern mantle-like 142 Nd/ 144 Nd in Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and Neoarchean komatiites, suggesting crustal and mantle sources that, at the time of extraction, had already homogenised isotope anomalies from Hadean Sm/Nd silicate fractionation events. We propose that slight µ 142 Nd variations between Williamstown Peridotite, Paringa Basalt, and Kambalda Komatiite samples from the Kalgoorlie Terrane resulted from assimilation of an ancient crustal component. Unlike 142 Nd patterns, many Yilgarn samples show clearly resolvable excesses in µ 182 W that range from +7.5 ± 3.0 to +10.3 ± 2.8 for Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and µ 182 W = +4.1 ± 0.8 (95% c.i., n = 11) for Neoarchean mafic-ultramafic sequences. The 182 W excesses likely record isotope signatures inherited from core-mantle differentiation and insufficient inmixing of late veneer material, preserved in the different mantle and crustal reservoirs related to the Yilgarn Craton. Overall, our results demonstrate that the Paleo- to Neoarchean mantle and crustal magma sources had been isotopically homogenised, with limited signatures from Hadean silicate differentiation but had not incorporated the complete late veneer budget of the modern mantle. Published: 2026-04-27T21:27:33+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.577 (consider)
item.content:encoded:

The Narryer Terrane is known for its Hadean zircons, demonstrating the existence of evolved Hadean crust in the Yilgarn Craton (W-Australia). However, the effects of Hadean material on long- and short-lived isotope signatures have remained ambiguous. We present the first combined 146 Sm- 142 Nd and 182 Hf- 182 W dataset for rocks from the Yilgarn Craton to identify putative Hadean components in crustal and mantle reservoirs through time. We investigated these short-lived isotope systems in mafic-ultramafic sequences, metasediments and granitoids covering the oldest preserved Paleoarchean rocks up to the Neoarchean granitoids that mark the stabilisation of the craton. Our study reveals modern mantle-like 142 Nd/ 144 Nd in Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and Neoarchean komatiites, suggesting crustal and mantle sources that, at the time of extraction, had already homogenised isotope anomalies from Hadean Sm/Nd silicate fractionation events. We propose that slight µ 142 Nd variations between Williamstown Peridotite, Paringa Basalt, and Kambalda Komatiite samples from the Kalgoorlie Terrane resulted from assimilation of an ancient crustal component. Unlike 142 Nd patterns, many Yilgarn samples show clearly resolvable excesses in µ 182 W that range from +7.5 ± 3.0 to +10.3 ± 2.8 for Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and µ 182 W = +4.1 ± 0.8 (95% c.i., n = 11) for Neoarchean mafic-ultramafic sequences. The 182 W excesses likely record isotope signatures inherited from core-mantle differentiation and insufficient inmixing of late veneer material, preserved in the different mantle and crustal reservoirs related to the Yilgarn Craton. Overall, our results demonstrate that the Paleo- to Neoarchean mantle and crustal magma sources had been isotopically homogenised, with limited signatures from Hadean silicate differentiation but had not incorporated the complete late veneer budget of the modern mantle.

item.title: Glacial dysoxia in the deep subpolar North Atlantic during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition
item.dc:creator: Iván Hernández-Almeida
Francisco Javier Sierro
Gabriel M. Filippelli
Antje H. L. Voelker
Paula Diz
item.description: Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT. Published: 2026-04-24T09:03:03+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Iván Hernández-Almeida; Francisco Javier Sierro; Gabriel M. Filippelli; Antje H. L. Voelker; Paula Diz

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-71268-4

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT.

item.title: Petrogenesis of pegmatites in the Shakhdara−Alichur dome, South Pamir (Tajikistan): Constraints from apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis
item.dc:creator: Murodov Azamdzhon
Amjad Hussain
Sharifjon Odinaev
Davlatkhudzha Murodov
Sherzod Abdulov
Amirkhamza Murodov
Xin Ma
Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda
Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol
Wei Dan
Gong-Jian Tang
item.description: The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:36+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.576 (consider)

Authors: Murodov Azamdzhon; Amjad Hussain; Sharifjon Odinaev; Davlatkhudzha Murodov; Sherzod Abdulov; Amirkhamza Murodov; Xin Ma; Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda; Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol; Wei Dan; Gong-Jian Tang

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b39007.1

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide.

item.title: Deciphering grain-size reduction as a driver of mid-lithosphere discontinuity formation
item.dc:creator: Mingqi Liu
Taras V. Gerya
Zhong-Hai Li
Ling Chen
James A. D. Connolly
item.description: The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution. Published: 2026-04-24T17:58:32+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.568 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mingqi Liu; Taras V. Gerya; Zhong-Hai Li; Ling Chen; James A. D. Connolly

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aed4229

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution.

item.title: Zinc isotope insights into the Archean continental crust stabilization: Evidence from potassic granites, Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons
item.dc:creator: Jianfeng Ma
Hamed Gamaleldien
Yuan-Ru Qu
Robert H. Smithies
item.description: Potassic granites are a major component of the Archean continental crust, yet their origin remains contentious due to uncertainties in source composition and melting conditions. We report integrated whole-rock geochemistry, Zn isotopes (expressed as δ 66 Zn relative to JMC-Lyon), and phase equilibria modeling for 3.22–2.65 billion years ago (Ga) potassic granites from the Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons, Western Australia. These granites show elevated K 2 O/Na 2 O (0.81–1.2) and variable δ 66 Zn (0.33 ± 0.03‰ to 0.50 ± 0.03‰, 2SD), overlapping those from the Kaapvaal Craton (δ 66 Zn from 0.30‰ to 0.46‰). Thermodynamic modeling indicates contrasting origins: Yilgarn granites formed by ∼10% partial melting of primitive tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) crust (K 2 O/Na 2 O ≤ 0.35, mantle-like zircon O isotopes), whereas Pilbara and Kaapvaal granites derive from 20 to 35% melting of evolved TTG crust (0.35 2 O/Na 2 O 18 O > 6‰). Heavy δ 66 Zn values (> 0.40‰) in a subset of potassic granites, especially where coupled with high Mg # (>40) and relatively low K 2 O/Na 2 O (<0.85), are most consistently explained by contribution from a carbonate-altered mafic source (e.g., carbonate alteration of oceanic crust), followed by 10–20% melting at 2–12 kbar and 640–850 °C. We propose that variations in source composition and partial melting conditions can account for much of the geochemical–isotopic diversity in the studied Archean potassic granites and identify Zn isotopes as a tracer of deep crustal recycling during early Earth continental stabilization and maturation. Published: 2026-04-28T15:30:34+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.567 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jianfeng Ma; Hamed Gamaleldien; Yuan-Ru Qu; Robert H. Smithies

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123456

Relevance: 0.567 (consider)

Potassic granites are a major component of the Archean continental crust, yet their origin remains contentious due to uncertainties in source composition and melting conditions. We report integrated whole-rock geochemistry, Zn isotopes (expressed as δ 66 Zn relative to JMC-Lyon), and phase equilibria modeling for 3.22–2.65 billion years ago (Ga) potassic granites from the Yilgarn and Pilbara cratons, Western Australia. These granites show elevated K 2 O/Na 2 O (0.81–1.2) and variable δ 66 Zn (0.33 ± 0.03‰ to 0.50 ± 0.03‰, 2SD), overlapping those from the Kaapvaal Craton (δ 66 Zn from 0.30‰ to 0.46‰). Thermodynamic modeling indicates contrasting origins: Yilgarn granites formed by ∼10% partial melting of primitive tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) crust (K 2 O/Na 2 O ≤ 0.35, mantle-like zircon O isotopes), whereas Pilbara and Kaapvaal granites derive from 20 to 35% melting of evolved TTG crust (0.35 2 O/Na 2 O 18 O > 6‰). Heavy δ 66 Zn values (> 0.40‰) in a subset of potassic granites, especially where coupled with high Mg # (>40) and relatively low K 2 O/Na 2 O (<0.85), are most consistently explained by contribution from a carbonate-altered mafic source (e.g., carbonate alteration of oceanic crust), followed by 10–20% melting at 2–12 kbar and 640–850 °C. We propose that variations in source composition and partial melting conditions can account for much of the geochemical–isotopic diversity in the studied Archean potassic granites and identify Zn isotopes as a tracer of deep crustal recycling during early Earth continental stabilization and maturation.

item.title: Polycyclic Metamorphism, Exhumation, and Recycling of Subduction Complex Rocks, Cedros Island, Baja California
item.dc:creator: Jordan W. Wang
Paul Kapp
Robert Holder
John He
David Hernández‐Uribe
James Worthington
item.description: Abstract High‐pressure rocks from subduction complexes are key records of the physical and chemical processes that operate on the subduction interface, but interpretation of these records requires accurate structural understanding of where they formed in the subduction zone and the mechanisms by which they were exhumed. We present new geologic mapping, outcrop‐scale observations, and geochronology from subduction‐zone assemblages at Punta Prieta Ridge on Cedros Island, Baja California (Mexico), to investigate the history of subduction, exhumation, and structural assembly of these rocks. The rocks of Punta Prieta Ridge are exposed in the footwalls of high‐ and low‐angle normal faults that carry Cretaceous forearc basin strata and attenuated mantle sections of the Cedros Island Ophiolite in their hangingwalls. The footwall rocks are subduction‐zone assemblages organized into distinct nappes that decrease in metamorphic grade and degree of strain structurally downward. Garnet‐amphibolite and blueschist blocks within the subduction complex yield 40 Ar/ 39 Ar cooling ages between 172 and 144 Ma and are hosted in siliciclastic rocks which yield detrital zircon maximum depositional ages between 92 and 72 Ma. Based on field evidence and age relationships, we interpret the occurrence of older higher‐grade blocks in younger clastic matrix to be depositional rather than the result of tectonic mixing. We then present a model for their exhumation and assembly via multiple cycles of extensional unroofing of the subduction complex and sedimentary recycling of high‐pressure rocks back into the subduction trench. We conclude by comparing the record of subduction on Cedros Island to other parts of the Cordillera. Published: 2026-04-24T06:45:13+00:00 Venue: Tectonics Score: 0.563 (consider)
